title: Finish a smart-pointer ownership conversion at an ABI boundary
prompt: Review and repair the ownership conversion in src/owner_api.cpp. The implementation was migrated from manually managed callback resources to unique_ptr-based owners, while raw resource handles remain as borrowed views for the stable C and C++ ABI adapters. The old constructor-failure cleanup path was retained: after a partial construction failure it destroys resources through those borrowed aliases, and normal C++ unwinding then destroys the smart-pointer owners again. Complete the conversion so every resource that was successfully acquired is destroyed exactly once on all paths. A channel must always be destroyed before its device, including configuration failure, and a failed acquisition must never be destroyed. Successful sessions must retain both resources until owner_session_destroy and return the original borrowed handle identities through both adapters. Preserve callback ordering, status mappings, null handling, all declarations and layouts in include/owner_api.h, and the mappings in include/owner_adapter.hpp. Do not modify the protected tests, public headers, Makefile, or .gitignore. Use only the C++ standard library; do not add network access, subprocesses, randomness, sleeps, or wall-clock behavior. Success is make test from the repository root.

full_distill.primary_skill: review a partial raw-owner to smart-pointer conversion and make one ownership authority govern constructor unwinding without changing borrowed ABI views
full_distill.baseline_evidence: make test exits nonzero because channel-creation and configuration failures invoke destroy callbacks through raw aliases and then invoke them again from unique_ptr member destruction
full_distill.success_contract: each acquired callback resource is destroyed exactly once in channel-before-device order on partial construction and normal destruction, while C and C++ ABI adapter signatures, layouts, status mappings, and borrowed handle identities remain unchanged
adversarial_factors: fake callback resources deliberately remain allocated after their first destroy event, turning a real double-free hazard into deterministic duplicate-callback evidence
device acquisition failure, channel acquisition failure, and post-channel configuration failure distinguish never-acquired, one-owner, and two-owner partial states
exact event sequences require reverse acquisition cleanup and reject fixes that merely suppress duplicate counters
successful construction checks that cleanup is deferred until explicit session destruction and that both ABI adapters expose the original borrowed identities
a separately C-compiled smoke test plus C++ signature and layout assertions protect the public ABI while malformed callback tables exercise existing validation
